title: Sorting with ORDER BY
description: Organize your chaos alphabetically or numerically.

slides.title: Alphabet Soup 🍜
slides.content:
# The chaotic database

By default, databases give you rows in the completely random order they were saved on the hard drive. That is rarely useful.

If you want a list of employees, you probably want them sorted Alphabetically. If you want a list of products, you want them sorted by Price.

slides.title: The ORDER BY Clause 🗂️
slides.content:
## Bossing the database around

To mandate a specific order, you affix the **`ORDER BY`** clause to the absolute very end of your query.

`SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY last_name;`

By default, this will magically sort everything in Ascending order (A-Z, 1-10).

quizData.question: If you don't use an ORDER BY clause, how does the database naturally return results?

options.text: Automatically alphabetical by the first column
In unpredictable, mostly random order based on how data is physically stored on the hard drive

quizData.explanation: You can never trust the default order of SQL results. If order matters to you, ALWAYS explicitly define it with ORDER BY.

slides.title: Going in Reverse ⏪
slides.content:
## DESC ending order

If you want the highest numbers first (like sorting by salary), or Z-A descending, you must attach the word **`DESC`** (short for Descending).

`SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;`

*(Note: While ASC is the default, you can explicitly write `ASC` for clarity).*

quizData.question: Which query correctly returns a list of employees starting with the HIGHEST earners?
quizData.explanation: Since we want the highest salaries at the top, we want the order to DESCEND from highest to lowest.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Select everything from `movies` and sort them from the newest `release_year` to the oldest.
